The **Copa Colombia** ([English](/wiki/English_language "English language"): Colombia Cup), officially known as **Copa BetPlay Dimayor**, is an annual [football](/wiki/Association_football "Association football") tournament in [Colombia](/wiki/Colombia "Colombia"). It is contested by the 36 professional clubs affiliated to the [División Mayor del Fútbol Profesional Colombiano](/wiki/Divisi%C3%B3n_Mayor_del_F%C3%BAtbol_Profesional_Colombiano "División Mayor del Fútbol Profesional Colombiano") (DIMAYOR) and is the nation's domestic cup competition, equivalent to the [FA Cup](/wiki/FA_Cup "FA Cup") in England or the [Copa del Rey](/wiki/Copa_del_Rey "Copa del Rey") in Spain.

The Copa Colombia was played for the first time in 1950, and it has been played consecutively since its revival in 2008. Throughout history, the **Copa Colombia** name has been used to refer to several tournaments or trophies:

* A [double-elimination](/wiki/Double-elimination_tournament "Double-elimination tournament"), knockout tournament parallel to the [Colombian league](/wiki/Categor%C3%ADa_Primera_A "Categoría Primera A"), which was played from 1950–51 to 1952–53.
* A tournament that was played under a round-robin group format in which teams advanced to a final group, which was also named as *Copa Presidente de la República*, played from late 1956 to early 1957.[[1]](#cite_note-1)
* A commemorative trophy awarded to [Millonarios](/wiki/Millonarios_F.C. "Millonarios F.C.") in 1963, which is not considered a Copa Colombia title.[[2]](#cite_note-#1-2)
* A tournament played in 1981 and 1989, which was part of the league season and awarded bonus points or berths to the final stages of the league tournament.[[2]](#cite_note-#1-2)
* The knockout tournament played by teams in [Categoría Primera A](/wiki/Categor%C3%ADa_Primera_A "Categoría Primera A") and [Categoría Primera B](/wiki/Categor%C3%ADa_Primera_B "Categoría Primera B"), established since [2008](/wiki/2008_Copa_Colombia "2008 Copa Colombia").[[3]](#cite_note-3)

[Atlético Nacional](/wiki/Atl%C3%A9tico_Nacional "Atlético Nacional") are the current holders, who won their [eighth Copa Colombia](/wiki/2025_Copa_Colombia "2025 Copa Colombia") title against [Independiente Medellín](/wiki/Independiente_Medell%C3%ADn "Independiente Medellín"), winning the final series by a 1–0 aggregate score. Atlético Nacional are also the most successful club in the competition.

## History

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Copa_Colombia&action=edit&section=1 "Edit section: History")]

### Copa Colombia in "El Dorado"

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Copa_Colombia&action=edit&section=2 "Edit section: Copa Colombia in \"El Dorado\"")]

The Copa Colombia was an idea promoted in 1950 by [DIMAYOR](/wiki/Divisi%C3%B3n_Mayor_del_F%C3%BAtbol_Profesional_Colombiano "División Mayor del Fútbol Profesional Colombiano") at the time known as "[El Dorado](/wiki/El_Dorado_(Colombian_football) "El Dorado (Colombian football)")" due to the boom experienced by football in the country. It emerged as an official tournament to be held annually, independent from the league championship and under a particular knockout format in which teams were divided by proximity into territorial groups of four teams each. Teams faced each other in home-and-away matches to determine the teams that advanced to a stage known as the winners' round. When a team lost two ties it was eliminated, while if it won it played a new round. The worst of the three remaining teams was eliminated, while a new tie was played, called losers' round, to determine the other team that reached the final. Due to the economic power of clubs and the Colombian league at that time, many teams had large payrolls for both tournaments as well as the international games they played. However, the little interest the competition generated among the participants led to delays in the scheduling of games and the first tournament ended in 1951.[[4]](#cite_note-CP-4) It was the reason why the next edition was not held until 1952, and its conclusion was again delayed until the following year.

In the period between both editions (1951–52), what turned out to be the early rounds of the 1952–53 edition is mistakenly referred as another edition of the competition due to its [double-elimination](/wiki/Double-elimination_tournament "Double-elimination tournament") format.[[5]](#cite_note-El_Cinco_Cero-5) [Boca Juniors de Cali](/wiki/Boca_Juniors_de_Cali "Boca Juniors de Cali") (as winners of the winners' round) and [Millonarios](/wiki/Millonarios_F.C. "Millonarios F.C.") (who were defeated by Boca Juniors in the winners' round but then went on to win the losers' round) played the final of the 1952–53 edition, which was ultimately won by Millonarios. Following that edition, the tournament was cancelled due to the lack of interest displayed by fans and teams alike.[[6]](#cite_note-Comutricolor-6)

An attempt to resume it was made in 1956–57: in said third edition, the first phase was played and six teams qualified to play a final hexagonal: [Atlético Nacional](/wiki/Atl%C3%A9tico_Nacional "Atlético Nacional"), [Santa Fe](/wiki/Independiente_Santa_Fe "Independiente Santa Fe"), [Deportivo Pereira](/wiki/Deportivo_Pereira "Deportivo Pereira"), Boca Juniors de Cali and the ones that would be the top two of said hexagonal: [Independiente Medellín](/wiki/Independiente_Medell%C3%ADn "Independiente Medellín") and [Atlético Bucaramanga](/wiki/Atl%C3%A9tico_Bucaramanga "Atlético Bucaramanga"); however, the grand final between these two teams was never played, thus the tournament was abandoned and no more editions were played until the 1980s.[[7]](#cite_note-7)

### Commemorative cup (1963)

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Copa_Colombia&action=edit&section=3 "Edit section: Commemorative cup (1963)")]

After consecutively winning the league titles in [1961](/wiki/1961_Campeonato_Profesional "1961 Campeonato Profesional"), [1962](/wiki/1962_Campeonato_Profesional "1962 Campeonato Profesional") and [1963](/wiki/1963_Campeonato_Profesional "1963 Campeonato Profesional"), [Millonarios](/wiki/Millonarios_F.C. "Millonarios F.C.") were granted permanent ownership of the "Copa Colombia" trophy, which should have been awarded to the winner of three editions. As these editions did not come to fruition, Millonarios were awarded the trophy on account of their league success.[[8]](#cite_note-8) Although the club counts it as a title, it is not recognized as an official title by DIMAYOR.[[5]](#cite_note-El_Cinco_Cero-5)

In 2017, and through its Twitter account, DIMAYOR included said trophy among those won by manager [Gabriel Ochoa Uribe](/wiki/Gabriel_Ochoa_Uribe "Gabriel Ochoa Uribe") (who coached the club in 1963), without clarifying whether it is an official title.[[9]](#cite_note-9)

### 1980s: Copa Colombia as part of the league tournament

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Copa_Colombia&action=edit&section=4 "Edit section: 1980s: Copa Colombia as part of the league tournament")]

In 1981, an event with the same name was held again but with the particularity that it was played by the 10 clubs eliminated from the final quadrangular of the [Campeonato Profesional](/wiki/Categor%C3%ADa_Primera_A "Categoría Primera A") (made up of 14 teams), as an appendix phase of the championship. Firstly, the six teams eliminated in the regular phase participated in a hexagonal from which one team ([Independiente Medellín](/wiki/Independiente_Medell%C3%ADn "Independiente Medellín")) qualified for the final, with the other finalist ([Deportivo Cali](/wiki/Deportivo_Cali "Deportivo Cali")) being decided in a quadrangular between the four teams that were eliminated in the semifinal phase. Independiente Medellín won the tournament after beating Deportivo Cali 3–1 in the first leg and a 1–1 draw in the second leg, both played in [Medellín](/wiki/Medell%C3%ADn "Medellín"). The competition had an incentive for the first time, awarding a spot in the league's final octagonal for the following season, which Independiente Medellín did not need in the end since they ultimately qualified through league performance.[[4]](#cite_note-CP-4) In 2014, this tournament was officially recognized by the [Colombian Football Federation](/wiki/Colombian_Football_Federation "Colombian Football Federation") as part of the tribute that [CONMEBOL](/wiki/CONMEBOL "CONMEBOL") paid to Independiente Medellín for its 101st anniversary.[[10]](#cite_note-10)[[11]](#cite_note-11)

In 1989, a new Copa Colombia edition was held, again as part of the [league championship](/wiki/1989_Campeonato_Profesional "1989 Campeonato Profesional"). It was played between the first and second rounds of the tournament and the points from this competition were added to the league's aggregate table, through which eight teams would qualify for the final stage of the championship. However, that year's tournament was abandoned due to the assassination of referee Álvaro Ortega. The Copa's first stage was played in three regional groups of five teams each, and the top eight in the tournament's table qualified for a knockout stage. [Santa Fe](/wiki/Independiente_Santa_Fe "Independiente Santa Fe") won the competition by beating [Unión Magdalena](/wiki/Uni%C3%B3n_Magdalena "Unión Magdalena") in the final, and was awarded 0.5 bonus points.[[12]](#cite_note-12) The runners-up, Unión Magdalena, were awarded 0.375 bonus points, whilst semi-finalists [Junior](/wiki/Atl%C3%A9tico_Junior "Atlético Junior") and [América de Cali](/wiki/Am%C3%A9rica_de_Cali "América de Cali") were awarded 0.250 and 0.125 bonus points, respectively.[[4]](#cite_note-CP-4)

### Reinstatement in 2008

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Copa_Colombia&action=edit&section=5 "Edit section: Reinstatement in 2008")]

On 14 February 2008, the 36 member clubs of DIMAYOR approved the holding of a new tournament known as Copa Colombia, integrating the teams competing in the [Primera A](/wiki/Categor%C3%ADa_Primera_A "Categoría Primera A") and [Primera B](/wiki/Categor%C3%ADa_Primera_B "Categoría Primera B") tiers of Colombian professional football. In its first editions, it granted the winner a berth into the following season's [Copa Sudamericana](/wiki/Copa_Sudamericana "Copa Sudamericana").[[13]](#cite_note-13)

Starting from its [2017 edition](/wiki/2017_Copa_Colombia "2017 Copa Colombia"), it granted its winner a place in the [Copa Libertadores](/wiki/Copa_Libertadores "Copa Libertadores") for the following season. The only exception to this was the [2020 edition](/wiki/2020_Copa_Colombia "2020 Copa Colombia"), which awarded its winner a berth into the [2022 Copa Sudamericana](/wiki/2022_Copa_Sudamericana "2022 Copa Sudamericana") given that the tournament was temporarily suspended due to the [COVID-19 pandemic](/wiki/COVID-19_pandemic_in_Colombia "COVID-19 pandemic in Colombia") and did not end within the calendar year. Qualification for the Copa Libertadores was granted to the winners until the [2023 edition](/wiki/2023_Copa_Colombia "2023 Copa Colombia"), with the 36 DIMAYOR member clubs deciding that the winners of the [2024 Copa Colombia](/wiki/2024_Copa_Colombia "2024 Copa Colombia") would qualify for the Copa Sudamericana.[[14]](#cite_note-14)
